local_names.romanized_local: Thayir Vadai (Tamil romanized); Dahi Vada (Hindi romanized)

region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu); widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Curd Vadai. Mapped to 'fritter (food product)' as the closest defensible parent class for a deep-fried lentil fritter. The yogurt-soaked composite nature is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (legume-based snack, deep-fried) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for curd vadai or dahi vada in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for curd vadai or equivalent fried lentil fritter dish. SNOMED CT does not cover this level of culinary specificity for South Indian snacks.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Curd Vadai consists of 2–3 deep-fried lentil fritters (approximately 250 g) generously soaked in spiced yogurt, providing roughly 380 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) — Dahi Vada / Urad Dal fritter composite
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for urad dal-based fried snacks and plain yogurt were used as the primary compositional basis. The per-100g composite reflects approximately 60% fried vadai and 40% yogurt by weight, consistent with the dish description. Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, calcium, iron, and fibre values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data. Trans fat and vitamin C values are supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried lentil products and plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 170903 and 01116). Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN fat composition data for urad dal fritters and yogurt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Curd Vadai (dahi vada) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database classification) based on: (1) urad dal (black gram) has a published GI of approximately 43 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) deep-frying raises the effective GI by disrupting the legume matrix and increasing fat-mediated gastric emptying effects; (3) yogurt soaking contributes a moderate glycaemic dampening effect via lactic acid and protein. The composite GI of 55 is consistent with analogous fried legume snack products in the University of Sydney database (e.g., fried falafel analogues, GI ~57). Per University of Sydney GI Database classification: low GI = ≤55, medium GI = 56–69, high GI = ≥70. GI 55 therefore falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (28.0 g from 250 g × 11.2 g/100g) / 100 = 15.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 260 mg/100 g; borderline sodium density with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Curd Vadai to one small serving (≤150 g) and avoid additional salty chutneys or condiments, as the cumulative sodium from the fried batter and spiced yogurt can approach 390 mg per serving, which is significant relative to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium GI (~55) from deep-fried lentil batter; dietary fibre only 1.6 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Curd Vadai in moderation (1 piece, ~80 g) due to its glycaemic index of approximately 55 and modest fibre content of 1.6 g/100 g, which provides insufficient glucose-buffering; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or requesting a baked preparation would reduce the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 10 g/100 g with saturated fat 3.2 g/100 g; deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Curd Vadai should be consumed sparingly due to its deep-fried preparation contributing 10 g total fat and 3.2 g saturated fat per 100 g, which can raise LDL cholesterol; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 8 g saturated fat, representing 40% of the recommended daily limit of 20 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried Curd Vadai as the high-temperature frying generates AGEs that exacerbate synovial inflammation; a steamed or baked vadai variant soaked in yogurt retains the anti-inflammatory benefits of urad dal, ginger, and cumin while eliminating the AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curd Vadai (also known as Thayir Vadai in Tamil or Dahi Vada in Hindi) is a popular South Indian snack widely enjoyed in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent. The dish consists of deep-fried lentil fritters (vadai) made from a spiced urad dal (black gram) batter, which are then soaked in chilled, creamy, spiced yogurt (curd). The vadai are typically seasoned with green chillies, ginger, cumin seeds, and curry leaves before frying, and the yogurt sauce is often garnished with fresh coriander, roasted cumin powder, and sometimes sweet tamarind chutney. The texture contrast between the soft, yogurt-soaked interior of the fritter and its slightly chewy outer layer is a defining characteristic of the dish. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 152 kcal, 6 g protein, 10 g fat (3.2 g saturated), 11.2 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 260 mg sodium. The calcium content of 72 mg/100 g reflects the yogurt component. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy. Clinically, the deep-fried preparation raises the fat content and glycaemic index relative to plain urad dal, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The yogurt component provides probiotic benefit and partially moderates the glycaemic response. Curd Vadai is commonly served as a snack, appetiser, or street food, and is a staple at South Indian festivals and family gatherings in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in curd vadai?
Is curd vadai good for diabetics?
Is curd vadai suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in curd vadai?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for urad dal-based fried snacks and plain yogurt, supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single approved-source entry exists for the exact composite dish (curd vadai / dahi vada with yogurt soak). The GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for urad dal and comparable fried legume products. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the curd vadai composite (fried urad dal fritter soaked in yogurt) is not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for the composite dish is not reported as a single entry in ICMR-NIN; estimated potassium from urad dal and yogurt components is approximately 180 mg/100 g but is not confirmed by a single approved-source composite entry and therefore not populated. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the specific curd vadai composite are not available as a single confirmed entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for curd vadai is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 = low; GI 56–69 = medium; GI ≥70 = high. GI 55 is therefore classified as low (gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in iteration 3 to align with University of Sydney GI Database boundary definitions). 5. The ethnic_tags 'Curry' and 'Naan' from the input record have been removed as they are not relevant to curd vadai. 6. The input source URL cited (ICMR-NIN DietaryGuidelines PDF) supports general Indian food composition context; the primary nutrient basis is ICMR-NIN component data for urad dal fried snacks and yogurt. 7. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
